title: Introducing MULAI: A Multimodal Database of Laughter during Dyadic Interactions
venue: Proceedings of the 12th Conference on Language Resources and Evaluation (LREC 2020)
research_questions: Introduce the Multimodal Laughter during Interaction (MULAI) database for studying expressive patterns of conversational and humour related laughter in dyadic human-human interactions.
How do self-rated humour and perceived humour by conversational partners correlate?
How do acoustic properties (pitch, intensity, duration) of laughter correlate with self-rated humour?
How do acoustic properties (pitch, intensity, duration) of laughter correlate with perceived humour?
tasks: survival task for conversational laughter elicitation
acoustic-property analysis of laughter and perceived humour

datasets_used.name: MULAI
datasets_used.size: 13 sessions; 357 minutes of recorded video-, audio- and physiological data streams; annotated part spans 134 minutes; 601 annotated laughter bouts, 168 annotated speech-laughs and 538 laughter related events; 26 participants

methods_proposed.name: MULAI database
methods_proposed.summary: A multimodal dyadic human-human interaction database designed to capture conversational and humour-related laughter, with synchronized audio, video, IMU movement, ECG, GSR, personality questionnaires, and participant self/perceiver humour ratings.

methods_proposed.name: Participant-context humour ratings linked to laughter acoustics
methods_proposed.summary: After each task, interlocutors rated how funny they thought they were and how funny they perceived their partner to be; the paper links these ratings to acoustic laughter features extracted from annotated laughter bouts.

humor_theories.detail: The discussion invokes the modified affect induction theory of laughter to interpret why perceivers may rely on social context and laugh intensity when judging humour.

evaluation_methods.detail: Participants provided five-point Likert self-ratings for the statement “I think I was funny” and perceived-partner ratings for “the other was very funny” after each task.

Laughter bouts, speech-laughs, and onset/offset respiration events were annotated in Praat using elements of the third tier of the laughter annotation scheme from Truong et al. (2019); unresolved cases were discussed among the authors.
Praat was used to extract duration, mean pitch, and mean intensity from annotated laughter bouts; Pearson rank correlation tests and linear mixed effects models were then applied in R.

human_samples.participant_count: 26 participants included in the database (32 participated over 16 sessions; 6 participants excluded because they did not give consent)
human_samples.population: Students from a course and participants they recruited from outside the course; age M = 24, SD = 2.3; 14 male and 12 female; most pairs were at least to some extent familiar; majority Dutch (N=17) with some international students; all participants spoke English.
human_samples.recruitment: Students from a course were asked to find a participant from outside the course and participate together in a data collection session.
human_samples.geography: University of Twente; majority Dutch (N=17) plus international students
human_samples.observations: 13 sessions of dyadic human-human interactions; 357 minutes recorded; 134 minutes annotated; 601 annotated laughter bouts, 168 annotated speech-laughs, and 538 laughter related respiration events.
human_samples.detail: Participants completed survival, make-the-other-laugh, and joke-telling tasks while wearing microphones, cameras, and Shimmer physiological sensors.

human_samples.population: The same dyadic interlocutors who participated in the MULAI recordings.
human_samples.geography: University of Twente; majority Dutch (N=17) plus international students
human_samples.observations: Five-point Likert humour self-ratings and conversation-partner ratings after each task.
human_samples.detail: Participants rated both their own humorousness and their partner’s humorousness, leveraging knowledge of the social context.

human_samples.observations: 240 video clips and 152 audio clips were segmented; 601 laughter bouts, 168 speech-laughs and 538 on-/offset respiration events were annotated for the make-the-other-laugh and joke-telling tasks.
human_samples.detail: A research assistant segmented audio/video using Adobe Premiere Pro. Laughter annotations were made in Praat; the number of laughter annotators and inter-annotator agreement are not reported.

judges.name: MULAI participant self-raters
judges.detail: Participants judged their own humorousness after each task using the five-point self-rating scale.

judges.name: Conversation partners / perceivers
judges.detail: Conversation partners judged each participant’s humorousness after each task. Participants were kept blind to their partner’s instructions for each make-the-other-laugh round; broader blinding is not reported.

judges.name: Research assistant/authors
judges.detail: Human annotators/experimenters segmented the recordings and annotated laughter bouts, speech-laughs, and laughter-related respirations; difficult cases were resolved through discussions among the authors.

judges.name: Praat
judges.detail: Praat-derived duration, mean pitch, and mean intensity values were used as acoustic predictors in the correlation analyses.

generation_selection.detail: The paper introduces and analyzes a human interaction database and performs no humor-generation experiment.

inference_budgets.detail: No computational model inference or LLM prompting is performed.

human_baselines.detail: The paper does not evaluate computational humor systems, so no human baseline against models is provided.

contamination_risk.basis: The paper does not evaluate LLMs or other models on a benchmark; it introduces a human interaction database and analyzes human ratings and acoustic features.

reporting_gaps: The number of laughter annotators and inter-annotator agreement for laughter annotations are not reported.
The exact number of completed humour questionnaire ratings used in the analyses is not reported.
The source, number, and language of the jokes distributed in the joke stacks are not specified.
The exact language(s) actually used in the recorded interactions are not stated beyond all participants speaking English.
No data URL or code URL is provided; access is described as requiring an individual license and contacting the authors.

headline_results.finding: MULAI contains dyadic human-human interaction recordings with a substantial annotated laughter subset.
headline_results.value: 13 sessions; 357 minutes of recorded video-, audio- and physiological data streams; annotated part spans 134 minutes; 601 annotated laughter bouts, 168 annotated speech-laughs and 538 laughter related events

headline_results.finding: The included database participants comprised a small young adult sample with more Dutch than international participants.
headline_results.value: 26 participants; age M = 24, SD = 2.3; 14 male and 12 female; majority Dutch (N=17)

headline_results.finding: Self-rated humour and partner-perceived humour were not significantly correlated in conversational tasks but were significantly positively correlated in joke-telling tasks.
headline_results.value: conversation ρ = 0.03, p = 0.527; joke-telling ρ = 0.22, p<.001

headline_results.finding: No duration, pitch, or intensity feature significantly correlated with humour self-ratings in either task setting.
headline_results.value: Conversation: duration p = 0.2447, pitch p = 0.0619, intensity p = 0.2543; joke-telling: duration p = 0.7500, pitch p = 0.2300, intensity p = 0.850

headline_results.finding: For conversation-partner humour ratings in the conversational task, laughter pitch and intensity were significant positive predictors.
For conversation-partner humour ratings in the joke-telling task, laughter duration was a significant negative predictor and intensity was a significant positive predictor.

key_findings: MULAI is a multimodal dyadic laughter database with audio, video, IMU movement, ECG, GSR, participant personality profiles, and participant humour ratings.
The annotated portion of the database covers 134 minutes and includes 601 laughter bouts, 168 speech-laughs, and 538 laughter-related respiration events.
Self-rated humour and partner-rated humour were significantly related only in the joke-telling task, not in the conversational task.
Laughter duration, pitch, and intensity did not significantly correlate with how humorous participants judged themselves to be.

Conversation partners’ humour ratings did correlate with acoustic laughter features: intensity in both task settings, pitch in the conversational task, and duration negatively in the joke-telling task.
The authors argue that the relationship between laughter acoustics and perceived humour appears task dependent and socially context-sensitive.
limitations: Speech-laughter was excluded from the preliminary analysis because of its complex and ambiguous nature.
The significant correlations reported in the discussion are described as small, and the authors note that other unexplored factors may show stronger correlations.
The authors state that they did not find an explanation in the literature for the negative correlation between laughter duration and perceived humour in the joke-telling task.
The survival task is not yet included in the annotations described for analysis, and data for this task is missing in three sessions.

Speech transcripts, strategies used to make the partner laugh, degrees of familiarity between pairs, and annotations of function/context for individual laughs are planned as future additions rather than reported as available.
The paper does not report inter-annotator agreement or the number of annotators for the laughter annotations.
safety_ethics_notes: Participants signed informed consent forms, and 6 participants were excluded from the database because they did not give consent for incorporating their data. Access to questionnaire, video, audio, and physiological data requires an individual license.

artifacts.other: The MULAI database is described as available to the research community after official publication; an individual license is needed and the paper asks readers to contact the authors for updates and availability.
